heuristics.rule: Plan 3–5 years ahead, not 6–12 months ahead
heuristics.condition: When making any decision about program, employer, or family that intersects with immigration
heuristics.action: Map out the immigration dependencies of your decision today — what visa becomes available, when, under what conditions — before committing
heuristics.because: Immigration systems have critical path dependencies; a wrong choice made years earlier (wrong program type, wrong employer) cannot always be corrected when the deadline arrives

heuristics.rule: Always have a Plan A, B, and C for each immigration stage transition
heuristics.condition: When approaching any status expiration or visa transition point
heuristics.action: Identify at least three viable pathways before the current status expires and know the trigger conditions for each
heuristics.because: Single-path planning means a single failure (e.g., lottery loss, employer layoff) leaves you with no legal recourse and no time to act

heuristics.rule: Verify an employer's actual sponsorship execution capacity, not just their stated willingness
heuristics.condition: When an employer offers verbal or written assurance they will sponsor your visa
heuristics.action: Ask specifically: how many employees have they sponsored, what immigration firm do they use, what is their approval rate, and who internally tracks your status deadlines
heuristics.because: Employers who have never executed a sponsorship frequently fail at the operational level — wrong filings, missed deadlines, wrong attorneys — even with good intentions

heuristics.rule: Spend $200–500 on an immigration attorney consultation before any major status decision
heuristics.condition: When facing a decision with real immigration stakes (program choice, job offer acceptance, status transition)
heuristics.action: Book a paid consultation with a reputable immigration attorney rather than relying solely on forums or peer advice
heuristics.because: The cost of a wrong status decision is typically measured in years lost and tens of thousands of dollars; a single consultation is a near-zero-cost risk mitigation

heuristics.rule: Reframe the stay-vs-return decision as a 10-year compounding question, not a current-conditions comparison
heuristics.condition: When evaluating whether to stay in the destination country or return home after graduation
heuristics.action: Compare career, financial, relational, and personal compounding trajectories over 10 years across both paths — including the full cost of immigration uncertainty
heuristics.because: Comparing current living conditions is emotionally loaded and systematically ignores the immigration stability tax and long-run trajectory differences between markets

heuristics.rule: Build an O-1 or EB-1 eligible track record continuously, not when you need it
heuristics.condition: When you are early in your career and have any possibility of needing an extraordinary ability visa later
heuristics.action: Document publications, awards, speaking invitations, and peer recognition consistently from early career stages
heuristics.because: O-1 and EB-1 eligibility requires a documented multi-year track record; you cannot retroactively manufacture the evidence when you urgently need the visa

heuristics.rule: Use immigration forums only to generate questions, not to make decisions
heuristics.condition: When researching an immigration question online via Reddit, Blind, or peer groups
heuristics.action: Treat forum answers as hypotheses to verify with an attorney, not as reliable guidance for your specific case
heuristics.because: Forum advice is subject to survivorship bias, outdated rules, and cannot account for the individual circumstances that determine actual immigration outcomes

decision_factors.why_it_matters: The ceiling, meritocracy, and compensation structure differ significantly between home country and destination country for specific roles — choosing the wrong market means slower career velocity for years
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Research senior-level roles in your field in both markets: compensation, equity availability, decision-making autonomy, and whether your nationality is an asset or a disadvantage at the top

decision_factors.why_it_matters: A high nominal salary in a destination country can generate less actual savings and wealth than a lower nominal salary in a lower cost-of-living home market
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate post-tax take-home in each market, subtract realistic cost of living, and compare savings rate and investment opportunity — not gross salary

decision_factors.why_it_matters: The mental energy, financial cost, and opportunity cost of maintaining and transitioning visa status is a real drag on career performance and life quality that is systematically underweighted in stay-vs-return decisions
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Estimate hours per year spent on immigration administration, attorney fees, and enumerate specific opportunities foregone due to visa restrictions (entrepreneurship, job switching, travel)

decision_factors.why_it_matters: An employer's stated willingness to sponsor is not the same as their operational capacity to execute — failure at the execution level has the same legal consequence as no sponsorship at all
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask for historical sponsorship data, the name of their immigration counsel, and their process for tracking employee status deadlines; evaluate the sophistication of their answers

decision_factors.factor: Program type and its downstream immigration eligibility
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The program you enroll in determines which post-graduation work authorizations and pathways are available — this must be assessed before enrollment, not after
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Before enrolling, confirm whether the program qualifies for STEM OPT extension (US), PGWP eligibility (Canada), and what visa categories it unlocks or forecloses

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Spouse immigration status, aging parents, and local family assets change the true cost-benefit of staying abroad in ways that are not visible in career-only analysis
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Explicitly map out each family member's situation: can a spouse work legally, what are the implications for aging parent care, does the family hold illiquid assets (property) in one country

decision_factors.factor: Emotional honesty about motivation for staying
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Decisions driven by fear of return being perceived as failure lead to suboptimal long-run choices; identifying the true motivation changes the analysis
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask directly: 'Would I make the same decision if returning home were socially celebrated rather than stigmatized?' If the answer changes, the decision is emotionally driven

decision_factors.why_it_matters: A job with better long-term immigration security (clear path to permanent residence) is often worth meaningful near-term trade-offs in salary or title because visa instability has compounding costs
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Map out the full immigration pathway from current status to permanent residence for each option; identify which steps depend on lotteries, employer cooperation, or rule changes

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying in the destination country on a work visa
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You systematically under-negotiate compensation, avoid career risks, and remain in unsuitable roles because your legal status is tied to your employer — creating 'golden handcuffs via immigration' that constrain behavior for years
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: All visa-dependent workers, but especially those in early career stages when risk-taking and job-switching are most valuable for career growth

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting a verbal sponsorship commitment from an employer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: If the employer has no immigration systems, no experienced counsel, and no internal process, the verbal commitment is worthless at the moment it matters most — and you may have no legal recourse
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students joining startups, small companies, or firms that have never sponsored before

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Relying on peer advice from online immigration forums
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You are exposed to survivorship bias (people share successes not failures) and receive advice calibrated to someone else's circumstances, which may not apply to yours — leading to confident wrong decisions
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Any student whose situation has unusual elements (specific nationality, niche NOC code, atypical program, employer type) where individual variation matters most

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Treating immigration planning as reactive rather than continuous
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Decisions made years earlier (program type, employer selection, country choice) foreclose pathways that cannot be reopened under time pressure — urgency eliminates options
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who made enrollment or employment choices without immigration pathway analysis and discover the consequences only when a deadline approaches

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Optimizing purely for salary when evaluating job offers as a visa holder
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: A higher-salary role with weaker immigration pathway security may generate less total value than a lower-salary role with a clear permanent residence trajectory, because visa instability has large opportunity and psychological costs
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who are analytically rigorous about compensation but do not quantify immigration risk in the same framework

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Not consulting an immigration attorney because it seems expensive
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The cost of a wrong immigration decision — lost years of work authorization, failed applications, deportation risk — is orders of magnitude larger than the $200–500 consultation fee
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Cost-sensitive students who economize on professional advice but are making five- and six-figure-impact decisions

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating immigration as a problem to solve when urgent rather than a system to navigate continuously
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Immigration feels abstract until a deadline is imminent; students are focused on academics and job search and defer immigration planning until it becomes a crisis
failure_patterns.consequence: Options that required early action (correct program enrollment, track record building, employer selection) are no longer available when the deadline arrives
warning_signs: Student cannot name their OPT end date or CRS score
Student has never researched the visa category they need to transition to next
Student's plan for the next status transition is 'I'll figure it out when I get there'

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting verbal or written sponsorship assurances without verifying execution capacity
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are relieved to have an offer and interpret 'yes we sponsor' as a solved problem; asking probing follow-up questions feels awkward or untrusting
failure_patterns.consequence: Employer fails to file correctly, misses deadlines, or discovers they cannot actually sponsor — the student loses their work authorization and may have no legal recourse
warning_signs: Employer cannot name the immigration attorney or firm they use
HR cannot answer how many employees they have previously sponsored
Nobody internally has been assigned to track your status deadlines

failure_patterns.pattern: Using peer immigration advice as decision-grade input for high-stakes choices
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Peers are accessible, relatable, and share confidently; consulting an attorney feels expensive and intimidating; forum answers feel authoritative when they match what you want to hear
failure_patterns.consequence: Student makes a major decision based on advice calibrated to someone else's nationality, program, employer, and timing — and discovers the discrepancy only when the outcome diverges
warning_signs: Student's primary research source is a Reddit thread or WeChat group
Student is citing a specific friend's experience as the basis for their plan
Student cannot identify how their situation differs from the peer they are referencing

failure_patterns.pattern: Framing the stay-vs-return decision as a comparison of current living conditions rather than long-run compounding
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Current conditions are vivid and available; 10-year trajectories require deliberate analysis and emotional honesty about motivation; fear of return being perceived as failure distorts the comparison
failure_patterns.consequence: Student stays for emotionally driven reasons, takes on years of immigration uncertainty and constraint, and ultimately reaches a worse long-run outcome than if they had returned strategically
warning_signs: Student frames return home primarily as 'giving up' or 'failure'
Student has not done a purchasing power or savings rate comparison between markets
Student cannot articulate a specific career advantage from staying beyond 'opportunities are better here'

failure_patterns.pattern: Making program and employer choices without assessing downstream immigration pathway implications
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students optimize for program prestige, salary, or immediate opportunity without the knowledge that these choices determine which visa categories they can access years later
failure_patterns.consequence: Student discovers their program does not qualify for the extension they needed, or their employer cannot support the next visa stage, after the decision is irreversible
warning_signs: Student chose program based solely on rankings or cost without checking immigration eligibility criteria
Student accepted an offer without researching the employer's visa sponsorship history
Student cannot describe the full immigration pathway from their current status to permanent residence

diagnostic_questions.question: Can you name your exact current visa status expiration date and the deadline for filing your next status transition?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is actively managing their immigration timeline or operating reactively
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What is your Plan B if your primary transition pathway fails — lottery loss, employer withdrawal, or denial?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: This is the most urgent priority before any other career or immigration planning; find out immediately and set calendar reminders 6 and 12 months in advance

diagnostic_questions.question: When you say 'my employer will sponsor me,' what specifically have they told you about their immigration attorney, filing timeline, and internal process?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the sponsorship commitment is operationally real or a well-intentioned but unexecuted assurance
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have they successfully sponsored someone at your visa category before — and can you speak to that person?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Treat the sponsorship as unconfirmed; ask the specific operational questions before relying on this employer as your primary immigration pathway

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you staying in this country because you genuinely believe it compounds better for you over 10 years, or because returning home would feel like failure?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the stay-vs-return decision is analytically grounded or emotionally driven by social framing of return as defeat
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Can you articulate specifically which career, financial, or personal factors compound better here — and have you run the purchasing power comparison?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: The decision warrants a structured reanalysis using a 10-year compounding framework before committing to another multi-year immigration cycle

diagnostic_questions.question: Before you chose your program and your employer, did you assess how those choices would affect your immigration options 3–5 years from now?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has already locked themselves into a constrained pathway through uninformed early decisions, or has navigational flexibility remaining
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What specific visa categories did you confirm were accessible given your program type and employer profile?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Map the downstream immigration implications of decisions already made immediately — earlier errors may still be correctable but the window closes quickly

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you had a paid consultation with a qualified immigration attorney (not a peer, not a forum) in the last 12 months?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has access to calibrated, situation-specific advice or is navigating a complex legal system using unreliable informal inputs
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What specific questions did you ask, and did they review your actual documents and timeline?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Prioritize a consultation before any next major decision; identify whether your situation has unusual elements that make peer advice especially unreliable

diagnostic_questions.question: Does your spouse or partner have independent immigration status, or is their status derivative of yours?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether a family dependency risk exists — if the student's status fails, the entire household's legal standing may fail simultaneously
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Does their independent status create additional pathway options (e.g., points contribution in Canada, alternative sponsorship route) that you have factored into your plan?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: The student's immigration planning must account for household-level risk; a single failure point affects multiple people and raises the stakes of every decision

diagnostic_questions.question: If your primary employer withdrew their sponsorship tomorrow, what would you do within the next 60 days to maintain legal status?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has a real contingency plan or whether all their immigration security is concentrated in a single employer dependency
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you verified that the contingency pathway is actually accessible to you — not just theoretically possible?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: This is a critical single point of failure; develop and verify at least one alternative pathway before it becomes urgent

industry_realities.common_belief: If an employer says 'yes we sponsor,' your immigration pathway is secure
industry_realities.actual_reality: Sponsorship willingness and sponsorship execution capacity are entirely different things; employers who have never navigated the process frequently fail at the operational level despite genuine intent
industry_realities.implication: Treat verbal and written sponsorship commitments as the beginning of due diligence, not the end; verify historical execution before making the commitment bilateral

industry_realities.common_belief: The best source of immigration advice is peers who have been through the same process
industry_realities.actual_reality: Immigration outcomes are highly dependent on individual circumstances — nationality, program type, employer, timing, application specifics — and people share successes far more than failures; peer advice has severe survivorship bias and calibration error
industry_realities.implication: Use forum research to generate the right questions, then pay for a qualified attorney to answer those questions for your specific situation

industry_realities.common_belief: The stay-vs-return decision is primarily about quality of life and career opportunity in the destination country vs. home country right now
industry_realities.actual_reality: The decision is primarily about which trajectory compounds more over 10 years across career, financial, relational, and personal dimensions — and the immigration stability tax on the stay path is consistently underweighted in informal analysis
industry_realities.implication: Force a structured long-run comparison that explicitly quantifies immigration costs (attorney fees, opportunity cost, mental energy, foregone entrepreneurship) before concluding the stay path is superior

industry_realities.common_belief: Immigration planning is something you do when an immigration deadline approaches
industry_realities.actual_reality: The decisions that most constrain your immigration options — program choice, employer selection, track record building — must be made years before the relevant visa deadline; by the time urgency arrives, the window for the best options has often closed
industry_realities.implication: Immigration planning must be integrated into every major career and education decision from the beginning, not treated as a separate later-stage problem

industry_realities.common_belief: A high salary offer is the most important variable when evaluating a job offer as a visa holder
industry_realities.actual_reality: The immigration pathway security embedded in a job offer — employer stability, sponsorship capacity, clear path to permanent residence — often has greater long-run economic value than marginal salary differences, because visa instability has large compounding costs
industry_realities.implication: Build an explicit immigration pathway analysis into every job offer evaluation and weight it alongside compensation, not subordinate to it

industry_realities.common_belief: Immigration decisions are primarily legal and logistical challenges
industry_realities.actual_reality: Immigration decisions are among the most emotionally loaded decisions people make — intersecting with identity, belonging, family, and perceived success — and the emotional loading systematically distorts analysis toward status quo bias and fear of return
industry_realities.implication: Explicitly separate the emotional and analytical dimensions of major immigration decisions; consult people who can challenge your reasoning, not just validate your preferences

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Plans 6–12 months ahead; treats the next visa stage as the entire problem; has no mental model of the 3–5 year pathway
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Plans 3–5 years ahead; maps the full dependency chain from current status to permanent residence before making near-term decisions; identifies which early choices foreclose later options
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Decisions made without a long-range map systematically create path dependencies that reduce options and increase costs at every subsequent stage

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Has one plan; assumes primary pathway succeeds; has not thought through what happens if the lottery fails, the employer withdraws, or the application is denied
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Has Plan A, B, and C for each stage transition; knows the trigger conditions for each; has verified that backup plans are actually accessible — not just theoretically possible
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Immigration systems have high variance — lotteries, employer instability, policy changes — and single-path planning means a single failure is catastrophic

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Takes 'yes we sponsor' at face value; does not probe operational capacity; discovers the execution gap only when a filing deadline is missed
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Asks specific due diligence questions before accepting: historical sponsorship volume, immigration counsel identity, internal tracking process, continuity through potential layoffs
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Sponsorship willingness and execution capacity are uncorrelated at small employers; a failed filing has the same legal consequence as no sponsorship at all

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Primary research is Reddit threads, Blind posts, or WeChat group advice; treats confident peer reports as reliable guidance for their own situation
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Uses forums to identify the right questions; validates situation-specific answers with a qualified immigration attorney; understands which aspects of their case are common vs. unusual
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Forum advice has survivorship bias and cannot account for the individual variation that determines actual immigration outcomes; confident wrong advice is worse than no advice

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Frames the decision as current quality of life comparison; conflates 'returning home' with 'failure'; does not quantify immigration stability tax or 10-year compounding trajectories
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Frames the decision as a 10-year compounding comparison across career, financial, relational, and personal dimensions; explicitly prices the immigration stability tax; interrogates whether the motivation for staying is analytical or emotional
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Emotionally framed stay decisions lead to accepting years of immigration constraint and opportunity cost that a rigorous analysis might not support

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Cannot readily state their current visa expiration date, the next filing deadline, or their CRS score; treats status management as the employer's or university's responsibility
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Knows exact status expiration dates 12+ months in advance; has calendar reminders for critical filing windows; tracks CRS score regularly if pursuing Canadian PR; owns their own timeline
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Missed deadlines and status violations are often irreversible; the individual bears the legal consequence regardless of whose fault the administrative failure was

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Track record building for advanced visa categories
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Is unaware of O-1 or EB-1 categories until urgently needing them; has not documented achievements, publications, or recognition; tries to retroactively build evidence under time pressure
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Understands which extraordinary ability visa categories might become relevant; documents publications, awards, speaking engagements, and peer recognition continuously from early career; builds the evidentiary record before it is needed
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: O-1 and EB-1 eligibility requires a multi-year documented track record that cannot be manufactured retroactively; early awareness creates optionality that late awareness forecloses
